findings.description: verify_checksum slurps the entire stored file into RAM via f.read() before hashing, allocating up to the full MAX_FILE_MB (200MB+) as one bytes object per call; should stream fixed-size blocks into hashlib.sha256().update() (constant ~64KB memory) instead.

file_size_ok is a post-assembly stat check, so the 200MB limit is enforced only AFTER every chunk is written to disk and concatenated, meaning chunk I/O and the verify_checksum full-read can already exhaust disk/memory before the size guard ever runs; the cap should bound accumulated chunk bytes during receive_chunk, not the final file.

receive_chunk writes caller-supplied chunk bytes with no per-chunk size cap, so a single oversized chunk is held fully in memory and written before any size check can intervene (memory/DoS-adjacent; bounding handled with critic-security).

assemble loops range(total_chunks - 1), so the final chunk is never concatenated, producing a truncated/corrupt output file; flagged briefly as the perf angle is wasted I/O writing a useless file, full triage deferred to critic-logic.
